metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: Replace 'resolve_current_identity (or the git-email call)' with a stated decision — either instrument BOTH call-sites with separate counters (preferred, since both are memoized), or state explicitly which one is the authoritative proxy and why.
findings.finding: AC5's monkeypatching target is stated as 'resolve_current_identity (or the git-email call)', leaving it to the executor's discretion which of the two cached operations to instrument. Since `attribution_fields` memoizes both the identity scan AND the git-email result in one cache entry, testing only one call-site counter does not prove the other is also cached — an executor who patches only the git call would miss a regression where identity resolution is called N times.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5 (Caching bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Assert call-count <= 1 on BOTH `resolve_current_identity` AND `_git_config` (or `author_email`) separately: `monkeypatch` both, do N=3 writes, assert each counter is <= 1. This proves the single memoization entry covers both operations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify §3: for `process_comment`, `process_revert`, and `process_signature`, specify whether `author_email`/`author_id` should be stamped INTO the sub-record dict (e.g. `_entry["author_email"]` in comments, the revert dict, the signature record) or added as a top-level `state["author_email"]` key — the current text says 'stamp at each processor that already stamps author' but none of these three touch `state["author"]` directly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `process_comment`, `process_revert`, and `process_signature` are processors that 'already stamp `author`' and instructs adding `state["author_email"]` / `state["author_id"]` there — but reading the implementation, none of these three processors set `state["author"]` (a top-level state key). They each set `author` inside a sub-record (`_entry` dict appended to `state["comments"]`; a revert-dict appended to `state["reverts"]`; the `record` dict stored in `state["signature"]`). If implemented as written, the plan's instruction would add a top-level `state["author_email"]` on every COMMENT/REVERT/SIGNATURE event — semantically wrong and not what bde5 needs — while missing the per-comment/revert/signature author_email that IS the natural home for this attribution.
findings.location: §3 (Replay back-compat), list of 'verified sites': process_comment (:248), process_revert (:374), process_signature (:494)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the flat list of four processors with two explicit groups: (a) `process_create` → set `state["author_email"]` / `state["author_id"]` on the ticket-level state (mirrors `state["author"]`); (b) `process_comment`, `process_revert`, `process_signature` → extend the per-event sub-record with `author_email` (and optionally `author_id`) present-only, matching the existing `"author"` key in each sub-record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/txn.py` to `file_impact` and stamp `attribution_fields()` onto STATUS and EDIT events in both `transition_core` and `claim_core`, mirroring the approach for delete.py's direct-envelope writes.
findings.finding: txn.py (transition_core + claim_core) is an unenumerated direct-write bypass: it builds STATUS and EDIT event envelopes directly without going through append_event, and is absent from file_impact. claim.py:169 and transition_close.py:372 call _seam.author() and pass it to txn.*_core, so every claim (open→in_progress STATUS + EDIT) and every transition (STATUS) will lack author_email/author_id, directly violating the 'every mutating event' AC.
findings.location: file_impact section; AC line 'Every mutating event's envelope carries author AND author_email'
findings.suggested_fix: In txn.py, import attribution_fields from _seam and merge its result into each event dict at construction time (same pattern as the planned delete.py fix). The tracker_dir is available as the first argument to both *_core functions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/txn.py` to `file_impact` and specify how `attribution_fields(repo_root)` is merged into the STATUS/EDIT envelopes built in `transition_core` (line ~276) and `claim_core` (line ~445), so claim and transition events also carry `author_email`/`author_id`.
findings.finding: txn.py (transition_core + claim_core) builds STATUS and EDIT event envelopes directly — bypassing append_event — and is not listed as a file to update. STATUS events written by claim and transition (which are mutating events) would not carry author_email or author_id, leaving a gap in the 'every mutating event' AC.
findings.location: ## What / ### 2. Stamp at the write seam(s) / file_impact
findings.suggested_fix: In txn.py, import `attribution_fields` from `_seam` and merge its result into the event dict alongside `author` at the same sites where `delete.py` is being updated (transition_core:276-284 and claim_core:445-453). The callers already pass `repo_root` indirectly via their call chains; thread it into claim_core/transition_core signatures as a keyword argument.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section 3 whether `author_email`/`author_id` are set at the TOP-LEVEL ticket state (only from process_create, mirroring `state['author']`) or also embedded into each COMMENT entry and REVERT record sub-dict (parallel to how `author` appears in those sub-dicts); remove process_comment and process_revert from the list of surfacing sites if only ticket-level attribution is intended.
findings.finding: The plan instructs 'set state["author_email"] / state["author_id"] ONLY when the event carries them' at process_comment (:248) and process_revert (:374), but those processors stamp `author` into sub-entry dicts (comment entries and revert records), not into top-level state. The plan's wording implies adding top-level ticket-state keys from COMMENT/REVERT events — which would mean the ticket's state['author_email'] reflects the *last commenter's* or *last reverter's* email, not the ticket's creator's email, creating a confusing semantic.
findings.location: ## What / ### 3. Replay back-compat / 'the verified sites are process_create (_processors.py:59), process_comment (:248), process_revert (:374), and process_signature (:494)'
findings.suggested_fix: If only ticket-level attribution is needed (for bde5 to read `author_id` from compiled state), restrict surfacing to process_create only — that is the only processor that sets state['author'] at the ticket level. process_signature, process_comment, and process_revert each embed author in their respective sub-dicts; if sub-entry attribution is desired, say 'add author_email to the _entry / record dict alongside author' rather than 'set state["author_email"]'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section 3 exactly WHERE each of the four processors places `author_email`/`author_id`: specify whether they go on top-level `state` (for process_create only, since the other three stamp `author` on sub-objects), on the sub-object entry (comment/revert/signature record), or both — and reconcile this with the AC requirement that 'the reduced state surfaces author_id/author_email' as top-level keys.
findings.finding: The placement rule 'set state["author_email"] / state["author_id"] ONLY when the event carries them, at each processor that already stamps author' is ambiguous because the four named processors stamp 'author' in structurally different locations: process_create puts it on top-level state['author'], while process_comment puts it on a comment-entry dict (inside state['comments']), process_revert puts it on a revert-record dict (inside state['reverts']), and process_signature puts it on the signature record dict. The plan does not specify whether the new author_email/author_id fields go on the top-level state dict, on the relevant sub-object, or both — yet the AC requires 'the reduced state surfaces author_id/author_email' (implying top-level), which would make the process_comment/process_revert/process_signature edits structurally incoherent (they write to sub-objects, not to state[]).
findings.location: Section 3 'Replay back-compat' — 'set state["author_email"] / state["author_id"] ONLY when the event carries them, at each processor that already stamps author'
findings.suggested_fix: Since only `process_create` writes to top-level `state['author']`, restrict Section 3 to: 'surface author_email/author_id on the top-level state at process_create (mirroring state["author"]); for process_comment/process_revert/process_signature, add author_email to the sub-object entry dict alongside the existing author key.' Update the AC to match: 'the reduced state top-level keys carry author_email/author_id (set by process_create); comment/revert/signature sub-records also carry author_email.'

findings.finding: The reducer surfacing instruction references '3-4 sites at `_processors.py`' that 'already stamp `author`', but the actual code has only one processor (`process_create`) that stamps `author` onto top-level state; the count and the list of target processors are undefined, leaving implementers without a precise edit target.
findings.location: Section 3 ('Replay back-compat') — 'Apply the present-only surfacing at each CREATE/edit/transition processor that already stamps `author` (the 3-4 sites at `_processors.py`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the cache key normalization rule in Section 1: e.g. 'resolve repo_root to its canonical absolute path (or a sentinel for None) before using it as the dict key, so None and the resolved absolute path for the same repo hash to the same entry.'
findings.finding: The memoization cache for `attribution_fields` is keyed 'per resolved repo_root' but the plan does not specify how the cache key is normalized when repo_root is None, a relative path string, a Path object, or an absolute path to the same directory — leaving a decision rule unspecified that determines whether the 'at most ONE git call + ONE ticket scan' invariant holds in practice.
findings.location: Section 1 'Shared git-config primitive + attribution helper' — 'MEMOIZE per resolved repo_root for the process lifetime (module-level dict)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 1: 'Normalize the cache key: resolve repo_root via `config.tracker_dir(repo_root).parent` (or equivalent) to a canonical absolute Path before keying the module-level dict, so None and an explicit absolute-path string for the same repo collide correctly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: Specify a concrete N (e.g. N=3 or N=5) for the sequential-writes caching test so the assertion is deterministic and non-trivially satisfiable.
findings.finding: AC5 uses the variable 'N sequential writes' without specifying N, making the criterion not fully measurable as written. Any N > 1 demonstrates the property, but an executor cannot write a deterministic test assertion without a concrete value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5 (Caching bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'do N=3 sequential writes in one process and assert the call-counter for each instrumented function is exactly 1'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the processor line-number citations in Section 3 to match actual current values: process_comment (~line 222/238), process_revert (~line 344/366), process_signature (~line 456/486), so implementers find the correct author-stamp lines.
findings.finding: The processor line numbers cited as 'verified sites' in the plan are all materially off: process_comment is at line 222 (plan says :248), process_revert is at line 344 (plan says :374), process_signature is at line 456 (plan says :494). An implementer following the plan's line references will land in the middle of wrong functions.
findings.location: What > Section 3 'Replay back-compat': 'the verified sites are process_create (_processors.py:59), process_comment (:248), process_revert (:374), and process_signature (:494)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Wrap the `resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root)` call inside `attribution_fields` in a `try/except Exception` (or at minimum `Exception`) so that any exception from identity resolution causes `author_id` to be omitted (graceful degradation) rather than propagating and aborting the write — mirroring the NEVER-raise contract already specified for `author_email()`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `author_email()` never raises (returns fallback on OSError/non-zero), but does NOT specify exception handling for `resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root)` inside `attribution_fields`. If `resolve_current_identity` raises (e.g., filesystem I/O error reading the identity ticket), the exception propagates through `attribution_fields` into `append_event` and `delete.py`, causing a write failure — breaking a mutation that has nothing to do with identity attribution.
findings.location: Section 1 — `attribution_fields` helper definition; AC line 'ONLY when resolve_current_identity(...) is not None'
findings.suggested_fix: In `attribution_fields`, wrap the call: `try: aid = resolve_current_identity(repo_root=repo_root); fields['author_id'] = aid if aid is not None else <omit> except Exception: pass` — ensuring any resolution failure silently degrades to no `author_id`, consistent with the NEVER-raise contract on the git helpers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2/AC4) asserting that `attribution_fields()` returns a dict with only `author_email` (no `author_id`, no exception) when `resolve_current_identity` raises — tested by monkeypatching it to throw — so the write seam is hardened against identity-store errors.
findings.finding: The plan adds `attribution_fields()` to the critical write path for every mutating event, but no AC tests the failure path where `resolve_current_identity()` raises an exception (as opposed to returning None). If it throws, every write in a process with a corrupted identity store would fail hard, breaking the "never raise" contract implied for helpers on the write seam.
findings.location: Section 1 (attribution_fields) and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: In Section 1, add to the attribution_fields spec: 'Wrap the resolve_current_identity call in try/except Exception; on any exception, treat as None (omit author_id and log a debug warning).' Then add to AC4 or AC2: 'When resolve_current_identity raises an arbitrary exception, attribution_fields() returns {"author_email": <value>} with no author_id and does not propagate the exception — tested by patching resolve_current_identity to raise ValueError.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact module path and call signature of `resolve_current_identity` once ddbe ships (or pin an interface contract in ddbe's AC that this plan can cite), and add an integration-order note to file_impact stating this ticket requires ddbe to be merged first.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the signature of `resolve_current_identity` is 'verified' as `keyword-only (*, repo_root=None)`, but the function does not exist anywhere in the codebase — ddbe (its source ticket) is still `in_progress`. This is an unverified capability assertion about an unshipped dependency interface; if ddbe ships with a different signature (positional arg, renamed param, or the module itself not landing at `rebar._commands.identity`), the `attribution_fields` helper will either fail at import or silently never populate `author_id`.
findings.location: Section 1 — attribution_fields helper: '`resolve_current_identity(*, repo_root=None)` per ddbe's implementation — verified, not the bare `()` the ddbe AC text abbreviated'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to file_impact / Scope: 'Depends on ddbe merging first; `attribution_fields` must be written/tested against the actual `resolve_current_identity` module path and signature, not the pre-merge assumption.' Optionally add a `try/except ImportError` guard in `attribution_fields` that returns `{}` (no `author_id`) when the identity module is absent, making the two tickets independently deployable.

findings.finding: The plan calls `resolve_current_identity(repo_root=...)` but the ddbe ticket spec never names `repo_root` as a parameter, and the function's source file is not in the repo snapshot — so the kwarg name and signature are unverified. A wrong kwarg (e.g. positional-only, or named differently) would TypeError at the single write seam on every write, silently breaking all mutations.
findings.location: What § 1 — 'resolve_current_identity(repo_root=...) is not None'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line signature contract to the plan, e.g.: 'Calls `resolve_current_identity(repo_root: str | Path | None = None) -> str | None` — verify the kwarg name against the ddbe implementation before merging this story.' Alternatively, add a brief import-and-introspect test that asserts `inspect.signature(resolve_current_identity)` includes `repo_root`.

findings.finding: The plan calls `resolve_current_identity(repo_root=...)` with a `repo_root` keyword argument, but the ddbe ticket's AC specifies the function signature as `resolve_current_identity() -> Optional[str]` — no `repo_root` parameter appears in the ddbe spec. This is a signature mismatch that would produce a `TypeError` at runtime.
findings.location: What / Section 1 — '`rebar._commands.identity.resolve_current_identity(repo_root=...)` is not None'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan: confirm (with a test or code reference) that old reducer code processes new events with unknown envelope keys without error, and document the rollback procedure if the change must be reverted after events with the new fields have been written.
findings.finding: The plan covers forward replay compat (old events → new reducer) but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan for the reverse direction: if the change is rolled back after deployment, new events already written with `author_email`/`author_id` in their envelopes must be safely processed by the old reducer. The plan never confirms the old reducer ignores unknown envelope keys without error, and provides no mechanism or procedure for rolling back.
findings.location: ## What § 3 (Replay back-compat) and ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section 'Rollback safety': note that all envelope processors use `.get(key)` / `event.get(...)` (verify this) so unknown keys are silently ignored, making rollback safe without a migration step; document this as the back-out path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the process-lifetime memoization decision (and its rationale, invalidation policy, and test-isolation implications) in a CLAUDE.md update, inline docstring contract, or a brief ADR entry so future maintainers understand the caching invariant.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — process-lifetime memoization of identity resolution cached in a module-level dict inside the write seam — with no accompanying ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry. This pattern has non-obvious implications (cache invalidation impossibility within a process, interaction with test isolation) that future maintainers need to understand.
findings.location: ## What § 1 (attribution_fields + MEMOIZE) and ## file_impact
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring to `attribution_fields` explicitly stating the memoization contract: 'Cached for the process lifetime per repo_root; call `_attribution_cache.clear()` in tests that change identity between writes.' Reference this in CLAUDE.md or an ADR stub.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the `attribution_fields` process-lifetime memoization cache with a concrete scale estimate or profiling result, or defer the cache until the batch-import path demonstrates a measurable hotspot (e.g. call `attribution_fields()` directly on each write and add caching only if the importer benchmarks show it as a bottleneck).
findings.finding: The module-level memoization cache on `attribution_fields` is a premature optimization: the plan cites 'a batch of N writes does at most ONE git call + ONE ticket scan' as justification, but provides no scale estimate, profiling result, or AC measuring performance. Per G-9, optimization requires affirmative scale evidence, not subject-matter inference. The cache adds implementation complexity (module-level dict, repo_root key normalization) without demonstrated need.
findings.location: What § 1 — 'MEMOIZE per resolved repo_root for the process lifetime (module-level dict)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either inline `_git_config` into `author()` and `author_email()` (avoiding a 2-call-site abstraction), or extend scope to also consolidate compact.py's `_git_author()` clone in the same PR to reach the Rule-of-Three threshold and justify the helper.
findings.finding: The `_git_config` helper is proposed as a shared primitive but will have exactly 2 call-sites within this plan's scope (`author()` reimplemented on top of it, and `author_email()`), since the plan explicitly bars refactoring the identical clones in compact.py and init.py. Two call-sites falls short of the Rule-of-Three threshold for a new abstraction layer.
findings.location: What § 1 — '_git_config(key: str, fallback: str = "") -> str primitive' / 'do NOT also refactor the unrelated clones in compact.py/init.py — out of scope'
findings.suggested_fix: Implement `author_email()` as a standalone function mirroring the existing `author()` body (same `subprocess.run` + strip + fallback pattern) without extracting `_git_config`. This keeps the diff minimal and avoids the premature helper. When compact.py is later in scope, extract `_git_config` at that point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback-state assertion to the plan: explicitly state that after a code rollback, compiled state for tickets with `author_email`/`author_id` events will silently drop those fields (pre-change reducer ignores unknown keys), that raw event data is preserved and re-surfaces on re-deploy, and confirm this is the accepted compensating-forward posture (no data loss, graceful degradation).
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the compiled-state shape AFTER a code rollback for tickets that already have events carrying `author_email`/`author_id`. T11 requires the plan to state the post-rollback schema state explicitly, not merely that rollback is safe.
findings.location: ## What § 3 (Replay back-compat) and ## Acceptance Criteria AC-3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback posture' note under the back-compat section: 'If this change is rolled back, events already written with author_email/author_id retain those fields in raw storage (append-only, immutable). The pre-change reducer ignores unknown envelope keys, so compiled state drops the fields silently — no crash, no data loss, full recovery on re-deploy. bde5/3183 consumers must tolerate absent author_id (already required by the present-only design).'

findings.finding: The reducer present-only surfacing described in §3 — that compiled state for a NEW event carries `author_email` (always) and `author_id` (when resolved) — has no covering acceptance criterion. AC bullet 1 tests only the RAW stored event JSON, not the compiled/reduced state. AC bullet 2 tests that OLD events reduce without new keys. Neither bullet verifies that a new event's reduced state actually contains `author_email`/`author_id`.
findings.location: §3 'Replay back-compat' / Acceptance Criteria bullet 1 and bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'A synthetic event carrying `author_email` and `author_id` fields, when passed through the reducer, produces compiled state where `state["author_email"]` and `state["author_id"]` equal the stamped values (verified by a unit test over `_processors.py`).`

findings.finding: The performance/memoization work described in §2 (memoize `author_email` and `author_id` per `repo_root` for the process lifetime) is a named deliverable with no covering acceptance criterion. None of the three AC bullets verify that caching occurs or that `resolve_current_identity` is called at most once per session for N sequential writes.
findings.location: §2 'Performance: resolve at most once per process' / Acceptance Criteria (all three bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 3 or add a new bullet: 'A batch of N (e.g. 3) sequential writes in the same process invokes `resolve_current_identity` exactly once (verified by mocking/patching the resolver and asserting call-count == 1), confirming the per-process cache is active.'

findings.finding: The `author_email()` helper's error-suppression contract ('returns \"\" when unset / the git call fails, never raises') is specified but no AC or test verifies the failure branch — leaving the silent-fallback behavior unvalidated and potentially locking in a silent failure if the implementation raises instead.
findings.location: AC block, first bullet; What §1 (author_email helper description)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a unit test: mock `subprocess.run` (or equivalent) to raise `FileNotFoundError` / return non-zero, call `author_email()`, assert result == `""` and no exception is raised. Also test the unset case (empty stdout).

findings.finding: The plan identifies the reducer sites needing `author_email`/`author_id` present-only surfacing as 'CREATE/edit/transition processors that already stamp `author`', but the actual code shows `process_edit` does NOT stamp `author` onto top-level state (it only applies field deltas from `data.fields`), and `process_status` (the transition processor) does NOT stamp `author` either. The real `author`-stamping processors are `process_create` (line 59), `process_comment` (line 240), `process_revert` (line 366), and `process_signature` (line 486). Targeting the wrong sites risks missing real stamp points or adding dead code to non-stamping processors.
findings.location: §3 'Replay back-compat' — 'Apply the present-only surfacing at each CREATE/edit/transition processor that already stamps `author` (the 3-4 sites at `_processors.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In §3, replace the parenthetical with: '(the 4 sites in `_processors.py` that call `event.get("author")`: `process_create` line 59, `process_comment` line 240, `process_revert` line 366, `process_signature` line 486 — `process_edit` and `process_status` do NOT stamp author and should be left unchanged)'

findings.finding: The plan is silent on the schema state after rollback: SNAPSHOT events written by the new code embed author_email/author_id in compiled_state, and process_snapshot restores all keys verbatim — so a rolled-back reducer will unexpectedly inherit these fields in compiled state for any compacted ticket, with no assertion or handling defined.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — back-compat bullet; also implicit in `process_snapshot` / `compact.py` interaction

findings.finding: `delete.py::_write_event` constructs the event envelope directly (bypassing `_seam.py::append_event`), stamping STATUS(deleted) and ARCHIVED events without going through the single seam — but the plan states attribution is stamped exclusively at `append_event` and does not address this existing bypass.
findings.location: Section 1 ('Stamp attribution at the single write seam'); `src/rebar/_commands/delete.py` lines 199-213

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
